name: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 02.03.2026

origin.filename: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 02.03.2026.pdf

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Rabi Sowing Status: Summer sowing data collection has been started from 9 th  February, 2026 and the sowing is in preliminary stage. As on February 27, 2026, the area sown under summer crops is 26.53 lakh ha as compared to 27.42 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.  Of this, area of 21.80 lakh ha has been sown under Rice this year.
texts.text: Rabi Sowing Status: Summer sowing data collection has been started from 9 th  February, 2026 and the sowing is in preliminary stage. As on February 27, 2026, the area sown under summer crops is 26.53 lakh ha as compared to 27.42 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.  Of this, area of 21.80 lakh ha has been sown under Rice this year.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  As on 27.02.2026, 99% of area has been harvested for Kharif crops all over India.
texts.text:  As on 27.02.2026, 99% of area has been harvested for Kharif crops all over India.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a) Temperature: Maximum temperature is likely to be above normal in NW India, East India, many  parts  of  NE  India, Chhattisgarh, Tamil  Nadu,  CAP,  Coastal  Karnataka,  KonkanGoa and some  parts  of  Saurashtra-Kutch. Maximum  temperature   is  likely  to  be below normal in many parts of Central India, Maharashtra, Telangana, Rayalaseema, and many parts of Karnataka and Kerala .
texts.text: Temperature: Maximum temperature is likely to be above normal in NW India, East India, many  parts  of  NE  India, Chhattisgarh, Tamil  Nadu,  CAP,  Coastal  Karnataka,  KonkanGoa and some  parts  of  Saurashtra-Kutch. Maximum  temperature   is  likely  to  be below normal in many parts of Central India, Maharashtra, Telangana, Rayalaseema, and many parts of Karnataka and Kerala .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: b) Rainfall Distribution: From March 01- March 02 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is 89%  lower  than  its  Long  Period  Average  (LPA),  with  regional  variations:  (i)  East  & Northeast India: (-) 63%; (ii) Northwest India: (-) 100%; (iii) Central India: (-) 100%; and South Peninsula: (-) 56%.
texts.text: Rainfall Distribution: From March 01- March 02 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is 89%  lower  than  its  Long  Period  Average  (LPA),  with  regional  variations:  (i)  East  & Northeast India: (-) 63%; (ii) Northwest India: (-) 100%; (iii) Central India: (-) 100%; and South Peninsula: (-) 56%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  A feeble Western Disturbance, light rainfall/snowfall likely over Jammu-Kashmir-Ladakh during 04th-07th March, 2026.
texts.text:  A feeble Western Disturbance, light rainfall/snowfall likely over Jammu-Kashmir-Ladakh during 04th-07th March, 2026.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Strong surface winds very likely over Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh during 02nd -03rd March.
texts.text:  Strong surface winds very likely over Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh during 02nd -03rd March.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Isolated  to  Scattered  rainfall  activity  likely  over  Western  Himalayan  region,  Kerala  & Mahe and Sub-Himalayan West Bengal & Sikkim.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Scattered to fairly widespread rainfall activity likely over Northeast India.
texts.text:  Scattered to fairly widespread rainfall activity likely over Northeast India.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Overall, rainfall is likely to be below normal over most parts of the country.
texts.text:  Overall, rainfall is likely to be below normal over most parts of the country.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Maximum  temperatures  are  likely  to  continue  to  be  appreciably  to  markedly  above normal by 4-7°C over many parts of Northwest India during next 5 days.
texts.text:  Maximum  temperatures  are  likely  to  continue  to  be  appreciably  to  markedly  above normal by 4-7°C over many parts of Northwest India during next 5 days.

texts.orig: a) Water  Reservoir  Status :  As  on  26  February  2026,  the  total  live  water  storage  in  166 important  reservoirs  across  the  country  (monitored  by  CWC)  is  108.338  billion  cubic meter,  which  is  59%  of  total  live  storage  capacity  (183.565  BCM). texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: b) Groundwater Levels :  In  August  2025,  In  Post-monsoon 2025, around 90% of the country's monitoring stations exhibited a depth to water level within 10 meters below ground level. Deeper  water  levels  of  more  than  20  m  are  observed  in  around  5%  of  stations  of  the country  covering  mainly  the  western  states,  especially  Western  Rajasthan,  Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh.
texts.text: Groundwater Levels :  In  August  2025,  In  Post-monsoon 2025, around 90% of the country's monitoring stations exhibited a depth to water level within 10 meters below ground level. Deeper  water  levels  of  more  than  20  m  are  observed  in  around  5%  of  stations  of  the country  covering  mainly  the  western  states,  especially  Western  Rajasthan,  Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh  during  Post-monsoon  2025  has  been  significantly  influenced  by  the  over extraction of groundwater compared to the recharge during the monsoon period.
texts.text:  The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh  during  Post-monsoon  2025  has  been  significantly  influenced  by  the  over extraction of groundwater compared to the recharge during the monsoon period.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Annual  water  level  comparison  with  the  previous  year  Post-monsoon  2024  to  Postmonsoon  2025  has  shown  that  about  63%  of  total  analyzed  stations  of  the  country experienced rise in  annual  water level fluctuation may  be  attributed to the less groundwater extraction compared to 2024.
texts.text:  Annual  water  level  comparison  with  the  previous  year  Post-monsoon  2024  to  Postmonsoon  2025  has  shown  that  about  63%  of  total  analyzed  stations  of  the  country experienced rise in  annual  water level fluctuation may  be  attributed to the less groundwater extraction compared to 2024.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  About 73% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level  in  the  decadal  mean  water  level  fluctuation  of  2015-2024  with  respect  to  Postmonsoon 2025.
texts.text:  About 73% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level  in  the  decadal  mean  water  level  fluctuation  of  2015-2024  with  respect  to  Postmonsoon 2025.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The  Groundwater  Drought  Indices  indicate  that  in Post-monsoon  2025 ,  groundwater conditions  across  India  remained  largely  stable,  with 91%  of  monitoring  stations showing no  deficit .  However,  localized  deficits  were  observed  in  several  regions  notably in Meghalaya, Nagaland, and the Dadar Nagar Havili & Daman & Diu.
texts.text:  The  Groundwater  Drought  Indices  indicate  that  in Post-monsoon  2025 ,  groundwater conditions  across  India  remained  largely  stable,  with 91%  of  monitoring  stations showing no  deficit .  However,  localized  deficits  were  observed  in  several  regions  notably in Meghalaya, Nagaland, and the Dadar Nagar Havili & Daman & Diu.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: c) Fertilizers : The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products.
texts.text: Fertilizers : The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products.
